speaker: interviewer
text: Wermuth, and this is the last tape. Would you like to introduce your family for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I've been on tape long enough not to introduce myself. This is my dear wife, Elisabeth. Behind me, my dear eldest daughter, Ilana.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And equally dear, least but-- last but not least-- Tricia-- [? Trisia, ?] I call her. And that makes up the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would one of you like to say what-- whether you feel your life might have been any different had your father not been a survivor and not lived through what he's lived through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who wants to speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you do. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's impossible to say. Some people say he has some eccentricities, [LAUGHS] but who knows if he would have had those had he not gone through the-- the horrors of the camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Other people say he's very normal. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've only ever had one father, so I honestly-- I couldn't say.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if you-- I mean, you cannot compare one person to another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if you were to compare him to fathers of some of your friends, would you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing that I notice more is the age difference. I don't know if the fact that he went through the camps made a difference, but he's definitely older than a lot of my friends' fathers, not that that makes any difference to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that's-- [LAUGHTER] --that is only because I married late.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I--

speaker: interviewee
text: It has nothing to do with the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I'm a balanced person, and therefore a comparatively normal person, and possibly--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --and possibly, contrary to what you said, I think I'm a younger person-- younger than most of your friends' fathers in my attitude and ways. Do you agree with that or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger daughter wouldn't agree with that, that much I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to add something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this point, no. [LAUGHTER] [INTERPOSING VOICES] It would take up too much tape, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: There will be no other point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just on a completely different tack, what was it that attracted you to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Impossible to say, but it was a gradual thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to know each other in some Jewish club.

speaker: interviewer
text: In London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I might as well tell the little story. [LAUGHS] I was sitting near the fire with another group, and two girls came in, amongst my-- them, my wife. I didn't, of course, know her. But she smoked at the time. And as she was looking around for an ashtray, there was none.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I approached her holding the coal bucket in my hand and said, sorry, this is the only ashtray we have around here. And that, more or less, was my introduction. So now you know how I met my wife. [LAUGHS] Different.

speaker: interviewee
text: Different.

speaker: interviewee
text: A great-- a great setup. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew then and there that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nothing that attracted you to her at that point.

words.text: you to her at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- no, no, no. I-- I liked her very much but I thought, you know, sort of, this is the marrying kind, and I'm not quite ready to get married yet. More or less.

speaker: interviewer
text: But eventually you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually I did, yes. We knew each other off and on for quite a number of years. Now, I would say, with regret, I should have married her-- and as I said in my book, I should have married her earlier, and so my children wouldn't have to complain they have an old daddy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but then we would have been older as well. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there you are. You can't have it all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't have it both ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Is there anything either of you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this point. Tell them how happy you are, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, we're-- we're very happy. We're a very happy family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you do look like a happy family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well-- [LAUGHS] at this stage-- if there's another stage, I don't know-- we haven't got much to add. Otherwise you can make a whole film of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's it. [? ELISABETH WERMUTH: ?] [? Thank you. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this particular picture, of course in much younger and happier days reminds me that I haven't got the bet-- best pictures because we left them behind when we went to-- we were-- we were deported to Poland. The biggest treasure they would be today. I have-- the few pictures I have are from relatives who managed to emigrate and took their albums with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Can you tell us who's in this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course there's my father on his motorbike. In the end he traveled for weeks and weeks on his motorbike in order to sustain a family. In front of him is my little sister, Hannah. [LAUGHS] Look, he can't-- he couldn't-- she couldn't take the sun, as I can see. Then my mother behind her. Well, it is my self. Now, this is my book, which I've written in five years of spare time, and it's called And this, I have explained before, these were the words my father said before we reached the gas chambers, on the way to the gas chambers. Breathe deeply, and get it over with quickly. Now, the picture is-- the illustration is from me, not a very, sort of, good one, but as good as I could. The memory of when I saw myself for the first time after liberation in a darkened window. The corpses which you see were actually 50 yards away, but on the picture I had to bring them nearer. OK, that's the one. Well, this is my beloved younger sister, Hannah, who in all my dreams-- dreams-- I thought she had the best chances to survive. Unfortunately, she could not overcome the first hurdle. But for details, read the story of the book. But she was about seven or eight on the picture, and of course one of the few hours, as you can see-- few happy hours she had in her life. Now this is my drawing of the house in Bochnia, where we stayed first four, then six in a room, and from where my mother and sister had been deported. The hiding place was on top of the porch, the triangle which you can see, which was only approachable from the back, from the rear, from the attic, which I had just covered with a piece of cardboard. They could look through the wooden plank in front, the crevices, and probably saw others go to the train. So in a way, they broke their promises not to go, and they went. Maybe they were threatened, but to our-- to-- to my knowledge, nobody went into the house. Now, this is a sketch map of the camp-- the Plaszow camp-- and it is out of the book of by permission of the publishers, Hodder and Stoughton. There are, according to my opinion, not enough barracks on it, and also the perimeter fence was further away from the metal workshops. And on the left of the picture, where the fence, at one stage in the middle, run into one, where we wanted to escape. Well, as the story goes and people have learned now, it is-- it was blocked by other-- in the last minute, by coils of more barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that is a picture taken in Rome, one of the pictures which was not given to me by relations because it was after the war. And, well, what should I say? Plaza Cinquecento and having gained about 100 weight at the time. So well, that's more or less it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is 31st of March, 1963, my wedding. My and my wife's wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 31st of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? Where, sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Well, the-- it was in New Oxford Street in a small restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, these are my two daughters. Well, I'm not sure what age they were, but obviously one can judge-- well, everybody can judge for themselves how old they were at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, my two lovely daughters. Now, this is the wedding of my oldest daughter, Ilana, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was it taken? On the 14th of April, 1991.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] 1991.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, on the 14th of April, '92-- '91, in the Cumberland. And, well, that more or less-- the younger one, at this very moment, is not married yet. [? There. ?] Now, this is the medal given to me by the Germans. And it says, 'In appreciation of my resistance against Nazi dictatorship.' Well, it was when they read that I tried to kill Hitler, and that was the result, when the German papers wrote about it.
